detection.regex: Error acquiring the state lock|timeout while waiting for state lock|Lock Info:|ID:\s+[0-9a-f-]{36}

solutions.title: Verify no other Terraform process is running
solutions.command_template:
# Check for active Terraform processes
ps aux | grep terraform

# If the process is dead and the lock is stale, force-unlock:
terraform force-unlock <lock-id>
solutions.explanation: The state backend (DynamoDB, GCS, S3) holds a lock to prevent concurrent modifications. If a previous `terraform apply` was interrupted (CI runner killed, network drop), the lock may be stale. First confirm no legitimate process holds it.

solutions.title: Force-unlock the stale state lock
solutions.explanation: Only run this after confirming no other Terraform process is active. Force-unlocking while another apply is running will corrupt the state file.

solutions.title: Add -lock-timeout to allow waiting
solutions.explanation: If multiple CI pipelines legitimately run Terraform concurrently, add `-lock-timeout` so they queue instead of failing immediately. Alternatively, serialize runs with branch protection or queue-based CI.

detection.regex: Error: No valid credential sources found|NoCredentialProviders|error configuring Terraform AWS Provider|google: could not find default credentials|ClientAuthenticationError|The client .* does not have authorization

solutions.title: Verify cloud provider credentials are set in CI
solutions.command_template:
# AWS — check these env vars are set:
echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
echo $AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
echo $AWS_DEFAULT_REGION

# GCP — check service account key:
echo $GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS

# Azure — check service principal:
echo $ARM_CLIENT_ID
echo $ARM_CLIENT_SECRET
echo $ARM_TENANT_ID
echo $ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID
solutions.explanation: Terraform providers authenticate using environment variables or credential files. In CI, these must be set as secrets. Missing or expired credentials trigger this error.

solutions.title: Use OIDC / Workload Identity Federation (recommended for CI)
solutions.command_template:
# GitHub Actions example (AWS OIDC):
# In your workflow:
- uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
  with:
    role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/GitHubActionsRole
    aws-region: us-east-1
solutions.explanation: Static long-lived credentials are a security risk. Use OIDC (GitHub Actions) or Workload Identity Federation (GCP) to get short-lived tokens — no secrets to rotate or leak.

solutions.title: Check IAM permissions for the CI role/user
solutions.command_template:
# AWS — simulate the policy:
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/CI-Role \
  --action-names ec2:DescribeInstances s3:PutObject
solutions.explanation: Authentication may succeed but authorization may fail. Check that the IAM role/service account has the required permissions for the resources Terraform manages.

detection.regex: Error: .* already exists|already exists, use terraform import|EntityAlreadyExists|BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou|ResourceAlreadyExistsException

solutions.title: Import the existing resource into Terraform state
solutions.command_template:
# Find the resource address in your .tf files, then:
terraform import <resource_type>.<resource_name> <provider-resource-id>

# Example — import an existing S3 bucket:
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.my_bucket my-existing-bucket-name

# Example — import an existing GCP GKE cluster:
terraform import google_container_cluster.primary projects/my-project/locations/us-central1/clusters/my-cluster
solutions.explanation: The resource exists in the cloud but not in Terraform state. `terraform import` reconciles state with reality without destroying/recreating the resource.

solutions.title: Use moved block or refactored resource address
solutions.explanation: If you renamed or restructured a resource in Terraform code, use a `moved` block (Terraform 1.1+) to tell Terraform the resource is the same — avoids destroy+recreate.

solutions.title: Delete the conflicting cloud resource manually (if expendable)
solutions.explanation: If the existing resource is not needed, delete it from the cloud console or CLI, then re-run `terraform apply`. Only do this for non-production, non-data resources.

patterns.name: Terraform Plan Will Destroy Many Resources

detection.regex: will be destroyed|Plan: \d+ to add, \d+ to change, \d+ to destroy|must be replaced|forces replacement

destroy_count.pattern: Plan: \d+ to add, \d+ to change, (\d+) to destroy

solutions.title: Review the plan output carefully before applying
solutions.command_template:
# Save the plan to a file for review:
terraform plan -out=tfplan.binary
terraform show tfplan.binary

# Filter just the destroys:
terraform show tfplan.binary | grep -A3 '# .* will be destroyed'
solutions.explanation: Unexpected destroys often indicate a resource rename, index change, for_each key change, or provider version upgrade. Never blindly apply a plan with unexpected destructions in production.

solutions.title: Use lifecycle prevent_destroy to guard critical resources
solutions.explanation: Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to databases, storage buckets, and other stateful resources. Terraform will error if a plan tries to destroy them, forcing a human review.

solutions.title: Use -target to apply only the safe changes
solutions.command_template:
# Apply only specific resources, skip the dangerous destroys:
terraform apply -target=aws_security_group.app -target=aws_lb.main
solutions.explanation: `-target` limits apply to specific resources. Use this to apply the safe additions/changes while you investigate the unexpected destroys. Note: -target is a workaround, not a long-term pattern.

detection.regex: Objects have changed outside of Terraform|Note: Objects have changed|drift|configuration was changed outside of Terraform

solutions.title: Run terraform refresh to sync state with reality
solutions.command_template:
# Terraform 0.15.4+: refresh is done automatically during plan
terraform plan -refresh=true

# To explicitly refresh state:
terraform apply -refresh-only
solutions.explanation: Drift occurs when someone modified a cloud resource outside of Terraform (manual console edits, scripts, other tools). `terraform apply -refresh-only` updates the state file to match current reality without changing infrastructure.

solutions.title: Identify and remediate the source of drift
solutions.explanation: Manual changes are a drift risk. Enforce Terraform-only changes via IAM policies that prevent direct console access to Terraform-managed resources. Consider enabling AWS Config or GCP Policy Intelligence for drift alerting.

solutions.title: Use terraformer or cf-terraforming to import drifted resources
solutions.command_template:
# Import individual drifted resources:
terraform import aws_security_group.app sg-0123456789abcdef0
solutions.explanation: If the drift is intentional (manual change you want to keep), import the modified resource so Terraform's state matches reality, then codify the change in your .tf files.

detection.regex: Error: Module not installed|Failed to query available provider packages|Could not retrieve the list of available versions|module .* not found|failed to download module

solutions.title: Run terraform init to download modules
solutions.command_template:
terraform init

# If using a module registry with authentication:
terraform init -upgrade
solutions.explanation: Terraform modules must be downloaded with `terraform init` before `plan` or `apply`. This is often the missing step in CI pipelines.

solutions.title: Check module source path and version constraints
solutions.command_template:
# In your module block, verify the source:
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"   # check this version exists
}

# List available versions:
curl https://registry.terraform.io/v1/modules/terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws/versions
solutions.explanation: The module may not exist at that registry path, or the specified version constraint may not match any published version. Check the Terraform registry for the exact source address and available versions.

solutions.title: Configure Terraform registry authentication for private modules
solutions.explanation: Private module registries require authentication. Set the token in the CLI config file or via environment variable in CI.

detection.regex: Error: Unsupported Terraform Core version|required_version|your Terraform version .* does not satisfy|This configuration does not support Terraform version

solutions.title: Switch to the required Terraform version using tfenv
solutions.command_template:
# Install tfenv:
brew install tfenv  # macOS
# or: https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv

# List available versions:
tfenv list-remote

# Install and use the required version:
tfenv install 1.7.0
tfenv use 1.7.0

# Or pin per-project via .terraform-version file:
echo '1.7.0' > .terraform-version
solutions.explanation: The Terraform binary version in CI doesn't satisfy the `required_version` constraint in your configuration. Use tfenv (or asdf) to switch versions, and commit a `.terraform-version` file to pin the version for the whole team.

solutions.title: Update the required_version constraint in your config
solutions.explanation: If you intentionally want to support a newer Terraform version, update the constraint. Use `>=` with an upper bound to avoid accidental breaking upgrades.

detection.regex: Error: .*(quota|limit) exceeded|QuotaExceeded|LimitExceeded|ResourceQuotaExceeded|you have exceeded your|QUOTA_EXCEEDED|TooManyRequestsException|Quota .* has been exceeded

solutions.title: Identify which quota is exceeded and request an increase
solutions.command_template:
# AWS — check service quotas:
aws service-quotas list-service-quotas --service-code ec2
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase \
  --service-code ec2 \
  --quota-code L-1216C47A \
  --desired-value 32

# GCP — check quotas:
gcloud compute project-info describe --project=my-project | grep -A2 quota

# Azure — check usage:
az vm list-usage --location eastus --output table
solutions.explanation: Cloud providers enforce per-account, per-region quotas. Identify the specific quota (e.g., vCPU count, EIP count) and request an increase via the provider's support console.

solutions.title: Deploy to a different region or use a different resource type
solutions.explanation: Quotas are per-region. If you need to deploy immediately, consider an alternate region or a smaller instance type that fits within existing quotas.
